mnemonics.title: Process States Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: NEW READY RUNNING WAITING TERMINATED - NEVER READY UNTIL READY TO WORK TIRELESSLY (N-R-R-W-T)

mnemonics.title: PCB Contents Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: PCB contains: STATE, PC, REGISTERS, MEMORY INFO, ACCOUNTING, I/O STATUS - S.P.R.M.A.I (Say Please Read My Assignment Instructions)

mnemonics.title: Scheduler Types Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: LONG-TERM = LOT (Load Only Ten jobs) - admission/degree of multiprogramming; SHORT-TERM = SST (Select Soon To run) - CPU selection

acronyms.usage: Data structure storing process information like state, PC, registers, and scheduling details
The main processor that executes process instructions
Unique identifier assigned to each process by the OS
Operations involving data transfer between CPU and external devices
System software managing hardware and process execution
Mechanisms for processes to communicate and synchronize
A scheduling algorithm that executes processes in order of arrival

visualTips.visualization: Think of a process like a person going through daily activities: Waking up (NEW) → Standing in line at a theme park (READY) → Riding a ride (RUNNING) → Taking a break when ride stops (BLOCKED/WAITING) → Leaving the park (TERMINATED)
Imagine a relay race runner (process) passing the baton (process state) to the next runner. The baton contains all the information needed to continue running from where the previous runner stopped.

Think of an animated flipbook: each page (process) has a slightly different image. Flipping quickly creates the illusion of motion (concurrent execution) when actually only one page is visible at a time (one CPU).
Picture a restaurant: Customers waiting to be seated (READY queue - need CPU) are different from customers waiting for their food to arrive (WAITING queue - need I/O event).

analogies.analogy: A recipe book (program) is passive, sitting on a shelf. When you follow a recipe to cook (process), you create an active instance with ingredients, utensils, and your attention - that's the process.
Just as a passport contains all your identity information when traveling, the PCB contains all essential information about a process needed by the OS to manage it.
The scheduler works like an air traffic controller at an airport, deciding which plane (process) gets to use the runway (CPU) next based on various priority rules.

When working on multiple homework assignments and switching between them, you must remember where you left off in each (save state) before starting another - that's exactly what context switching does for processes.
